data.textItem: Shape sorter: more shapes

Welcome to the shape-sorting factory!

We need your help to program Sirc, our robot,
to sort shapes into sets.

Select a door to begin.
Shape pairs
Single shapes
Single shapes

Before you program Sirc, you need to work out how he should sort the shapes.

In this part of the factory, we make sets of shapes that share geometric features (for example, the same number of sides).

First, make a Checklist to help you work out the features to look for.
Checklist for sorting
Select the four most useful questions to ask about each shape:
How many sides does the shape have?
What colour is it?
Does it have rotational symmetry?
Are its sides straight or curved?
How many angles does it have?
What is the name of the shape?

That's not it.

We're not going to sort the shapes
by colour.

That's not it.

We're not going to sort the shapes
by name.

That's not it.

We're not going to sort the shapes by colour or name.

That's still not it.

We don't need to know the colour of the shape or its name because changing these features doesn't change the shape.

Oops!

That still wasn't right.
Shapes

This is a useful checklist!

In our factory, shapes that belong to a set are
called YES shapes. You need to work out what
they have in common.

Select Clues to investigate YES and NO shapes.

Now work out what a new set of shapes has in common.

Select Clues to investigate some new YES and NO shapes. Your checklist will still be helpful!

Checklist for sorting
What makes YES shapes belong together?

Features to check:
• How many sides does the shape have?
• Does the shape have rotational symmetry?
• Are its sides straight or curved?
• How many angles does it have?
What do YES shapes have in common?

• To find clues, select YES shapes to see examples
of the shapes in a set. Select NO shapes to
explore the shapes that don't belong.

• Choose a shape and use the Rotate tool to
explore its features.

• Don't forget to use your Checklist if you need to!

• To find clues, select YES shapes to see examples
of the shapes in a set. Select NO shapes to
explore the shapes that don't belong.
• Choose a shape and use the Rotate tool to
explore its features.
• Don't forget to use your Checklist if you need to!

YES
shapes

NO
shapes
Select a shape to investigate using the Rotate tool.
When you've worked out what all the YES shapes have in common, go to the factory.
Explore each shape!
Rotate tool

• First Copy the shape.
  • Next Slide the copy over the original shape.
  • Then Rotate it to find out how many times the copy matches the original when it is turned.
Counts straight sides only, not curves
Shows 'Yes' only if all sides are straight and are the same length

All sides equal?

Yes/No
Counts right angles inside the shape only

Right angles

0/1/23/4/5/6/
Counts all the lines of symmetry in all types of shapes

Lines of symmetry

0

1/2/3/4/5/6
Records each time the copy matches the original when rotated.
Checklist for sorting

What makes YES shapes belong together?

Features to check:
• How many sides does the shape have?
• Does the shape have rotational symmetry?
• Are its sides straight or curved?
• How many angles does it have?

Have you discovered what the YES shapes have
in common?

Test if you're right by sorting some shapes.
Keep any YES shapes and Shred any NO shapes.

Use the Rotate tool if you need to.

Wait! That's a YES shape.

Select Clues to check the feature it has in common with the other YES shapes.

Wait! That's a NO shape.

Select Clues to try to work out how this shape is different from the YES shapes.

Wait! That's a YES shape.

Select Clues to check the feature it has in common with the other YES shapes.

Wait! That's a NO shape.

Select Clues to try to work out how this shape is different from the YES shapes.

Hold on! That's a YES shape.

Select Clues to compare this shape's rotational symmetry with the other YES shapes.

Hold on! That's a NO shape.

Select Clues to compare this shape's rotational symmetry with the other YES shapes.

Hold on! That's a YES shape.

Select Clues to compare this shape's rotational symmetry with the other YES shapes.

Hold on! That's a NO shape.

Select Clues to compare this shape's rotational symmetry with the other YES shapes.
Stop! That's a YES shape because it has rotational symmetry with exactly four matches.
Stop! That's a NO shape because it does not have rotational symmetry with exactly four matches.
Stop! That's a YES shape because it has rotational symmetry with exactly two matches.
Stop! That's a NO shape because it does not have rotational symmetry with exactly two matches.
Clues for single shapes

• To find clues, select YES shapes to see examples
of the shapes in a set. Select NO shapes to
explore the shapes that don't belong.
• Choose a shape and use the Rotate tool to
explore its features.
• Don't forget to use your Checklist if you need to!
The YES shape you tried to shred
The NO shape you tried to keep
First select a shape to examine with the Rotate tool.

Excellent!

You picked five YES shapes that fit into a set.

You are now ready to train Sirc.
Train Sirc

We want Sirc to carry on the job of making sets
of shapes.

Program him with the directions for how to pick
YES shapes.
Select the statement that is true for this set of YES shapes, and Upload it to program Sirc.
Select the statement that is true for this set of YES shapes, and Upload it to program Sirc.
A YES shape must have:

It's time for Sirc to sort the shapes!

Let's see how well you programmed him.

Wait! That's a YES shape.

Select Program to retrain Sirc.

Wait! That's a NO shape.

Select Program to retrain Sirc.

Look at the YES shapes again.

Then select the statement[!s] that [!is/are] true for all the YES shapes in this set.

Still not correct.

Read the statements carefully. Then select the statement[!s] that [!is/are] true for this set of YES shapes.

You correctly selected one statement that is true.

To fully describe our YES shapes you need to select another statement.

That still isn't right.

Once the correct statement has been chosen for you, select Upload to reprogram Sirc.
YES shapes
straight sides only
rotational symmetry with exactly four matches
sides that are all the same length
four right angles
straight sides only
exactly two lines of symmetry
rotational symmetry with exactly two matches
sides that are all the same length
angles that are all the same size
straight sides only (that is, the shape must be a polygon
exactly one line of symmetry
no lines of symmetry

That's it! You've trained Sirc to find shapes with
one feature in common.

These YES shapes belong in the same set
because they all have:
• rotational symmetry with exactly four matches.

That's it! You've trained Sirc to find shapes with
one feature in common.

These YES shapes belong in the same set
because they all have:
• rotational symmetry with exactly two matches.

Thanks for teaching Sirc how to sort single shapes with one common feature!
• Select Shape pairs to program him to sort pairs into sets.

• Go to Single shapes again to help him sort shapes with two common features.

Thanks for teaching Sirc how to sort shape pairs!
• Select Single shapes to program him to sort single shapes into sets.


• Go to Shape pairs again to help him sort different pairs of shapes.

Before you program Sirc, you need to work out how he should sort the shape pairs.

In this part of the factory, we make sets of shape pairs that share geometric features.

First, make a Checklist to help you work out the features to look for.
Checklist for sorting
Select the four most useful questions to ask about each shape pair.
Do the shapes have angles of the same size?
Are both shapes the same colour?
Do the shapes have sides of the same length?
Has one of the shapes been rotated?
Do both shapes have the same name?
Are the shapes the same size?
Shape pairs

This is a useful checklist!

In our factory, shape pairs that belong to a set are called YES pairs. You need to work out what the pairs in a set have in common.

Select Clues to investigate YES and NO pairs.

Now work out what a new set of shape pairs has
in common.

Select Clues to investigate some new YES and NO
pairs. Your checklist will still be helpful!

Checklist for sorting
What makes YES pairs belong together?

Features to check:
• Do the shapes have angles of the same size?
• Do the shapes have sides of the same length?
• Has one of the shapes been rotated?
• Are the shapes the same size?

• To find clues, select YES pairs to see examples of
pairs in a set. Select NO pairs to explore the pairs
that don't belong.

• Use the Modify tool on each pair to explore the
features they have in common.

• Don't forget to use your Checklist if you need to!

• To find clues, select YES pairs to see examples of
pairs in a set. Select NO pairs to explore the pairs
that don't belong.
• Use the Modify tool on each pair to explore the
features they have in common.
• Don't forget to use your Checklist if you need to!

YES
pairs

NO
pairs
Select a shape pair to investigate using the Modify tool.
When you've worked out what all the YES pairs have in common, go to the factory.
Modify tool
Select a button to modify the shape on the right. See how it compares to the other shape in the pair.
Checklist for sorting

What makes YES pairs belong together?

Features to check:
• Do the shapes have angles of the same size?
• Do the shapes have sides of the same length?
• Has one of the shapes been rotated?
• Are the shapes the same size?

Have you discovered what the YES pairs have
in common?

Test if you're right by sorting some pairs.
Keep any YES pairs and Shred any NO pairs.

Use the Modify tool if you need to.

Wait! That's a YES pair.

Select Clues to check what it has in common with the other YES pairs.

Wait! That's a NO pair.

Select Clues to check how this pair is different to
the YES pairs.

Wait! That's a YES pair.

Select Clues to check what it has in common with the other YES pairs.

Wait! That's a NO pair.

Select Clues to check how this pair is different to
the YES pairs.

Hold on! That's a YES pair.

Select Clues to compare this pair's features with those of the other YES pairs.

Hold on! That's a NO pair.

Select Clues to compare this pair's features with those of the YES pairs.
Stop! That's a YES pair because the two shapes have the required feature: angles of the same size, but one shape has longer sides.
Stop! That's a NO pair because the two shapes do not have the required feature: angles of the same size, but one shape has longer sides.
Stop! That's a YES pair because the two shapes have the required features: the same shape, but one has been enlarged, and both are in the same position.
Stop! That's a NO pair because the two shapes do not have the required features: the same shape, but one has been enlarged, and both are in the same position.
Clues for sorting

• To find clues, select YES pairs to see examples of
pairs in a set. Select NO pairs to explore the pairs
that don't belong.
• Use the Modify tool on each pair to explore the
features they have in common.
• Don't forget to use your Checklist if you need to!
The YES pair you tried to shred
The NO pair you tried to keep
First select a pair to examine using the Modify tool.
What do YES pairs have in common?
Explore each pair!

Excellent!

You picked five YES pairs that fit into a set.

You are now ready to train Sirc.

We want Sirc to carry on the job of making sets
of shape pairs.

Program him with the directions for how to pick
YES pairs.
Select the statement that is true for this set of YES pairs, and Upload it to program Sirc.
Select the statements that are true for this set of YES pairs, and Upload them to program Sirc.
A YES pair must have:
two shapes that are exactly the same
two shapes in the same position
two shapes with angles of the same size, but one has longer sides
one shape with some smaller angles than the other shape
two shapes that are the same, but one has been enlarged
both shapes in the same position
two shapes with angles of the same size and sides of the same length
two shapes that are exactly the same, but one has been rotated

It's time for Sirc to sort the shapes!

Let's see how well you programmed him.

Wait! That's a YES pair.

Select Program to retrain Sirc.

Wait! That's a NO pair.

Select Program to retrain Sirc.

Look at the YES pairs again.

Then select the statement[!s] that [!is/are] true for all the YES
pairs in this set.

Still not correct.

Read the statements carefully. Then select the statement[!s] that [!is/are] true for this set of YES pairs.

You correctly selected one statement that is true.

To fully describe our YES pairs you need to select another statement.

That still isn't right.

Once the correct statement has been chosen for you, select Upload to reprogram Sirc.

That still isn't right.

Once the correct statements have been chosen for you, select Upload to reprogram Sirc.
YES pairs

That's it! You've trained Sirc to find shape pairs
that share features.

These YES pairs belong in the same set
because they all have:
• two shapes with angles of exactly the same
size, but one shape has longer sides.

That's it! You've trained Sirc to find shape pairs
that share features.

These YES pairs belong in the same set
because they all have:
• two shapes that are the same, but one has
been enlarged, and both are in the same
position.

Now Sirc is trained to sort single shapes into sets! Thanks for all your help!

These shapes belonged together because they had rotational symmetry with the same number of matches.

Now Sirc is trained to sort pairs of shapes into sets! Thanks for all your help!

When two shapes are the same, except one has been enlarged, they are called similar shapes.
Affirmative!
